text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're talking about one of my favorite applications of robotic and MIS approaches and that's in the management of patients with subxiphoid hernias. This was a four-year-old man with a history of aortopathy related to their connective tissue disease. a history of NUS procedure for pectus abnormality and aortic root reconstruction via an extended sternotomy.

He presented with a bulge in like the lower portions of their chest and CT scan had been performed demonstrating herniation of preperitoneal and falciform fat through the lower aspect of the sternotomy incision that had violated the abdominal wall fascia. Hernia characteristics, the defect was relatively small, but had a large volume of herniated contents, mostly made up of fat. The hernia sac extended about ten centimeters from the inferior aspect of the sternum xiphoid region.

The surgeon begins the preperitoneal dissection in the falciform region, revealing herniated contents and part of the defect near the sternotomy incision. The robotic approach is used to visualize and manipulate the tissues, aiming to reach the central tendon of the diaphragm.

The surgeon gains access in the upper abdomen and places ports strategically. During the pre-peritoneal dissection, the hernia area is reached, revealing expected herniated fat. The scrub nurse applies manual pressure to assist in reducing the herniated contents.
The surgeon addresses a multifocal defect, sequentially reducing multiple hernias along the midline. The first defect is reduced, revealing large volumes of herniated fat.

The surgeon performs a standard dissection, aggressively reducing the herniated fat with minimal adhesions. The xiphoid process is visible, and the incarcerated contents have been fully dissected and reduced. The surgical team prepares to develop a healthy space for mesh placement.

The surgeon carefully checks for any viscera near the surgical field before proceeding with subdiaphragmatic dissection. The central tendon of the diaphragm becomes visible, and the surgeon uses cautery to manage small bleeders while continuing to identify the central tendon.

The final view shows approximately seven centimeters from the superior aspect of the defect for mesh coverage. The surgeon begins closing the defect, opting for a transverse closure for the smaller defect and planning a vertical closure for the upper defect.

The surgeon successfully brings the tissue together without major issues, opting not to perform any myofascial releases. A heavyweight mesh measuring fifteen by eighteen centimeters is placed to provide circumferential coverage around the small hernia defects.
The surgeon performs a straightforward peritoneal closure using absorbable sutures. The camera is positioned close to the surgical field, requiring careful maneuvering to avoid collisions.

The surgical field shows a significant volume of pre-peritoneal fat that has been reduced. The operation site appears hemostatic, with no drain placement. The patient is prepared for same-day discharge.
